utterances: You have to buy a whole pound?
A few blocks away, we found a store that sells nothing but buttons.
So, what is the cheapest button in the store?
We definitely have to find something smaller.
Same block as the button store is Trim De Carnival, a store that sells tiny sequins.
You know, the things you sew on a dress for, say, Mardi Gras.
We walked in and asked Krishna Verna(ph): What is the cheapest thing we can get?
Well, you could get a sequin for, like, a penny.
The bag is $5 or whatever, you know, and then a single sequin could be, like, a penny.
But if somebody came in with a penny and said open that up.
You just told me it was a penny.
And we should say here, it's not like she's that busy.
Mardi Gras was a couple of months ago.
Selling something for a penny is not worth Verna's time.
The button guy said the space a button takes up in a Manhattan store costs him more than a penny.
There are super-cheap things in the world - like a little sequin - but people didn't want to sell them to us.
At 38th Street Hardware, we found this little flimsy little electrical nut.
So, why not just sell it for, whatever, a penny, two cents.
UNIDENTIFIED WOMAN #1: It's not worth selling.
I'd rather keep my customer and just give it to them.
The value of the good will she gets from giving away the electrical nut is worth more than the few cents she'd sell it for.
I'm going to spoil the suspense, here.
Bottom line: no one would sell us anything for a penny.
But we came as close as you can possibly come.
This here is a little metal washer with a hole which you put with a screw to help hold it in place.
Jim Bengtson is one of the brothers, and he's showing us a tiny washer.
By far, the cheapest thing we've found in Manhattan.
Do we know who makes this?
What is the name of your company?
Ningbo Exact Fasteners Company Limited of Ningbo, China, specializes in making the washers like the one we bought.
We called them because we wanted to know: how could you sell this thing for two cents?
Think of all that has to happen to get this washer to a hardware store in Manhattan.
You have to dig the metal out of the ground, turn it into steel, make the steel into a washer, package it, get it onto a ship and send it across the world.
And then you have to get it off the ship.
You have to get it to the store, put on the shelf in that little box, and all of this for two cents.
The workers at Exact Fastener make about $700 a month, Jim told us.
And, of course, no one has a patent on washers, so lots of companies are competing to sell washers as cheaply as possible.
And probably the biggest reason washers are so cheap: volume.
Ningbo Exact Fastener Company Limited sells washers by the ton.
And I don't mean this is a metaphor.
I asked Jim, how many washers do you get in a ton?
Seven hundred and twenty thousand, yes.
Seven hundred and twenty thousand washers?
If you buy a ton of washers, one penny will get seven of the little guys.
Factor in shipping, a tiny bit of profit for everyone along the way, and you get what just might be the smallest transaction you can make in New York City.
